# Trucktrack partitioning

## Tier by trip extent

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Cleaned trips are bucketed by bbox diagonal — picks the Valhalla tile size we shard against, so a longhaul is never chopped into many tiny partitions.

| Tier | Diagonal | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `local` | < 100 km | T → Mississauga |
| `regional` | 100–800 km | T → Ottawa |
| `longhaul` | > 800 km | T → Vancouver |
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figures/tier-classification.png){.nostretch}
:::
:::

## Partition cells

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
A partition key is `(tier << 60) | tile_id`. The tile is computed at the tier's level using Valhalla's grid math, anchored on a representative point of the trip.

Reading "all local trips through Mississauga" → one partition lookup. "All longhaul trips through southern Ontario" → a few. The same trip never lives in two partitions.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/partition-cells.png){.nostretch}
:::
:::

## Hilbert order inside a partition

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
Inside one partition, trips are sorted by a Hilbert index over their anchor point.

The Hilbert curve is a 2D → 1D mapping that keeps spatially close points close in the linear order. Trips physically near each other end up adjacent on disk, so a small file scan pulls trips from a small geographic patch — warming the same Valhalla tile cache entries for the next match.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/hilbert-locality.png){.nostretch}
:::
:::

## Why this stack matters

- **Tier** keeps partitions roughly balanced across trip sizes
- **Tile** lines partition boundaries up with what Valhalla loads
- **Hilbert** keeps adjacent reads spatially adjacent

Without any of the three, batch map-matching either over-partitions (many small files, lots of opens) or under-partitions (one giant file, no parallelism, and a cold tile cache for every record).
